segments.s369-g89: This clause has an inverted, poetic word order: the place(s) (در و دشتها) are fronted, followed by the past copula شد 'became', and then the predicate adjective همه لاله‌گون 'all tulip‑coloured'. همۀ quantifies the predicate (meaning 'entirely/altogether'), and لاله‌گون is a predicative adjective describing the state they became. Note: در here is a place‑noun in the poem (not the preposition 'in'); the line uses a coordinate list of places (در و دشتها).
segments.s369-g90: به دشت و بیابان is a prepositional phrase marking direction/extent ('into/over the plains and open country'). همی + رفت forms an archaic imperfective/past progressive (همی is a poetic prefix indicating ongoing action; رفت is past of رفتن), so 'kept going/flowing'. خون is the moving theme ('blood') and appears after the verb for poetic word order, functioning as the thing that 'went'.
segments.s369-g91: This is the first half of a 'so ... that ...' (چنان ... که) construction. چنان 'so' + بد used adverbially/intensifying ('very/badly') express high degree; ز بس کشته is a causal/genitive phrase 'from/owing to so many slain'. The noun آن رزمگاه (that battlefield) comes at the end by poetic inversion and is the locus being described.
segments.s369-g92: که introduces the resulting clause ('that ...'). بد before می‌توانست weakens ability (i.e. 'barely'/'with difficulty'). می‌توانست is the past potential ('could'), and رفتن (the masdar/infinitive) is the verbal complement meaning 'to go/travel'. به راه 'on the road' is the directional complement. Together they mean 'one could hardly go along the road' (i.e. passage was difficult).
segments.s369-g73: سرانجامش آمد is a past narrative clause: سرانجام (here adverb) = “at last/finally,” with the enclitic -ش referring back to the person previously mentioned (“to him”). آمد is the past tense “came.” یکی تیر چرخ is a noun phrase following the verb (typical poetic word order): یکی = “a certain/one,” تیر = “arrow,” چرخ = “heaven/sky” (the source of the arrow). So literally: “At last to him came a certain arrow of the sky.”
segments.s369-g74: چنان آمده بودش is a perfect-pluperfect construction: چنان = “thus/so,” آمده = participle “having come / come,” and بودش is بود + enclitic -ش used here as the auxiliary ‘had’ with the same third-person reference (’to him/it had been’). از چرخ برخ is a short prepositional phrase: از = “from,” چرخ = “the heavens/sky,” and برخ is a poetic/contracted form meaning “came forth/issued” (from برخاست). Together: “it had thus come forth from the heavens.”
segments.s369-g75: بیفتاد is a past-tense verb “fell” (third person). زان = از آن “from that/from it.” شولک = a small cloak/garment; خوب رنگ is a descriptive compound where خوب (well/fine) qualifies رنگ (color/appearance) — together “well-colored” or “handsome-colored cloak.” Word order is inverted for poetic effect: Verb + prepositional phrase + noun phrase.
segments.s369-g76: بمرد here functions as a past narrative form meaning “he died” (poetic usage); و is the conjunction “and.” نرست is a past/negative-coded form meaning “did not rise/grow/return” (here: did not recover or rise again). اینت is an archaic/dialectal form standing for “this is/this was” (demonstrative linking to what follows). فرجام جنگ = “the outcome/end of the battle.” The clause states the result: he died and did not rise; this was the battle’s end.
segments.s369-g77: دریغ at the clause head is an exclamatory particle meaning “alas/pity.” آن = “that/the.” سوار = “rider/horseman.” گرانمایه is an adjective meaning “precious/esteemed” (here describing the rider). نیز = “also/too,” marking that this rider, too, is lamented. The sequence is an exclamatory noun phrase: “Alas that esteemed rider as well.”
segments.s369-g78: که introduces a relative clause (“who/that”). افگنده شد is a passive construction: افگنده (participial/verb root ‘cast/thrown’) + شد (auxiliary ‘was’), so “was cast/thrown.” رایگان is an adverb meaning “for nothing/without cause.” بر is the preposition “upon/on,” and چیز literally means “thing” — together in this line they form an idiomatic phrase meaning “cast down for no reason/for nothing.” Grammatically: a relative passive clause modifying the rider.
segments.s369-g79: که again introduces a clause: همچون پدر بود = “he was like his father” (همچون = “like,” پدر = “father,” بود = past tense ‘was’). و همتای اوی = “and his equal/counterpart”: همتای is a noun ‘equal’ with the possessive/enclitic اوی referring back to the person (‘his’). Word order places simile first then adds the parallel predicate “his equal.”
segments.s369-g80: دریغ آن begins another exclamation: “alas that….” نکو روی is a compound adjective phrase: نکو = “good/fair/noble,” روی = “face” → “fair-faced” or “good-looking/noble-faced.” و = “and.” بالای اوی: بالای here denotes “highness/status” (literally ‘above’) and اوی is the possessive enclitic “his,” so “his high rank/lofty station.” Together the phrase laments his appearance and high standing.
segments.s369-g49: بیامد is a simple past verb ('came'); هم is an adverb here meaning 'also' (emphatic addition). آنگاه is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards' and بستور is an adverbial/epithet (classical/poetic form) meaning 'thus/in this fashion' used to introduce the epithet that follows; شیر is a noun placed after those adverbs as an appositive epithet ('the lion'). Word order is verb — adverb(s) — epithet, which is typical in older Persian poetic diction.
segments.s369-g50: This string is a sequence of nominal epithets/attributive phrases modifying the subject (Bostūr). نبرده is an archaic negative participial/adjectival form ('not-taken' → here 'untaken' or 'undefeated'); کیان زاده is a compound meaning 'born of the Kian(s)' (i.e. of the royal house); پور is the stock word for 'son/descendant' and زریر is a proper name in apposition. The whole segment lists genealogical/heroic epithets after the name.
segments.s369-g51: بکشت is an archaic past-formation meaning 'killed' (the prefix ب- + past-stem), with او as the third-person pronoun functioning as the (implied) object 'him'. ازان is an older form of از آن ('from those/of those') introducing the group; دشمنان بی‌شمار is the noun phrase 'countless enemies' — together it means 'one of those countless enemies.'
segments.s369-g52: که here is the relative pronoun 'who/which/that' introducing a relative clause. آویخت is a past verb 'hung' or figuratively 'was stricken/seized' (i.e. 'was thrown into / caught by'); اندر is the older preposition 'in/within'; بد modifies روزگار to mean 'ill/bad fate' — together: 'who were caught in ill fortune.'
segments.s369-g53: سرانجام is an adverb 'at last/finally.' برگشت is the past verb 'returned.' پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives ('victorious and glad') that describe the subject on return; the coordinate ו (و) links the two adjectives.
segments.s369-g54: به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of (or before) his father' with به + پیش; باز شد here uses باز ('again') with شد as an intransitive past (classical usage) meaning 'appeared / presented himself' (i.e. 'came before'); و ایستاد is a simple past 'and he stood' (stopped/stood in the father's presence). The clause sequence gives motion/arrival plus the resultant posture.
segments.s369-g55: بیامد is past 'came.' پس here is an adverb meaning 'afterwards' or 'then' that sequences the narrative; آن برگزیده سوار is the noun phrase 'that chosen rider/horseman' (آن + adjective برگزیده + noun سوار). In heroic poetry such noun phrases immediately follow the verb for dramatic arrival.
segments.s369-g56: پس is again a temporal/conjunctive adverb 'then/after that.' شهریار جهان is a title 'king (or lord) of the world' (شهریار governed by جهان). نیوزار here appears as an appositional name or epithet placed after the title; in context it functions as the name of the ruler. (Note: نیوزار in the provided sense list is given as a landscape term; in the poem it reads as a proper name/apposition.)
segments.s369-g81: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” introducing the clause. کشته شد is a passive construction: کشته (past participle) + شد (past of شدن) = “was killed.” آن نامبرده سوار is a nominal group ‘that aforementioned rider/horseman’ with آن (demonstrative) + نامبرده (aforementioned) qualifying سوار (noun).
segments.s369-g82: ز گردان is a prepositional phrase ‘from (the) troop/ranks’ (ز = from, گردان = troop). به گردش is a directional/phase phrase ‘into a turning/movement’ (به = into/towards, گردش = turn/movement). هزاران هزار is a hyperbolic numeral ‘thousands upon thousands’ describing the number that moved; word order places the motion phrase before the numeral for emphasis.
segments.s369-g83: بهر گوشه‌ای is a directional phrase ‘to/for every corner’ (بهر = to/toward, گوشه‌ای = a corner / each corner). بر هم آویختند is a verbal phrase meaning ‘they hung/lay upon one another’ or ‘were heaped together’ (بر = on/upon, هم = together/on each other, آویختند = past plural of آویختن ‘to hang/attach’).
segments.s369-g84: ز روی زمین is a locative phrase ‘from/on the surface of the ground’ (ز = from, روی = on/upon, زمین = ground). گرد انگیختند is the verb phrase ‘they raised dust’ (گرد = dust, انگیختند = past plural of انگیختن ‘to arouse/raise’), with the verb in past plural.
segments.s369-g85: برآمد is a past-tense verb ‘arose/occurred.’ برین here is a compound of بر + ین (an older/poetic form corresponding to این) giving ‘this’ or ‘on this,’ so برین رزم کردن = ‘this fighting arose/was begun.’ دو هفت is a time expression literally ‘two sevens,’ used here as a poetic way to indicate a period of two sevens (i.e., two weeks or fourteen days).
segments.s369-g86: کز ایشان is a relative/partitive phrase ‘of/from them’ (کز = that/which/from which used to introduce the clause, ایشان = them). سواری زمانی نخفت means ‘not a single rider slept for a while’: سواری = rider/horseman, زمانی = for a while (temporal), and نخفت is the past form of خفت(ن) with initial ن indicating negation here, so ‘did not sleep.’
segments.s369-g87: زمینها (plural) ‘the lands/grounds’ is the subject. پر از + noun = ‘full of …’ so پر از کشته و خسته شد = ‘became full of the slain and the weary.’ کشته و خسته is a coordinated noun phrase (the slain, and the exhausted/worn), and شد is the past copular/auxiliary meaning ‘became/was.’
segments.s369-g88: سراپرده‌ها is a plural noun ‘the inner chambers/inner tents.’ نیز is the adverb ‘also/too.’ بربسته is a compound participial adjective ‘shut/closed up’ (بر + بسته), and شد is the past auxiliary ‘became/were’—together ‘were likewise shut.’
segments.s369-g65: سواران چین پیش او تاختند is a simple past clause: سواران چین (a noun phrase) = “the Chinese riders/horsemen” (modifier چین follows سواران to mark origin/association). پیش او is a prepositional phrase “in front of him / at him.” تاختند is a 3rd-person plural past verb (classical/past narrative) meaning they charged/attacked, so the order is Subject — adverbial phrase — Verb.
segments.s369-g66: برافگندنش را همی ساختند contains a verbal noun with an attached pronominal object: برافگندن (to cast/throw down) + ـش (’him’) → برافگندنش “throwing him down/its throwing-down.” را is the definite/object marker. همی before the verb gives an imperfective/continuous shade in classical Persian. ساختند is a past plural verb here meaning “set about / made / did,” so the phrase means they set about bringing him down.
segments.s369-g67: سوار جهانجوی مرد دلیر is a stacked nominal phrase with epithets: سوار (rider/horseman) + جهانجوی (a compound adjective/epithet “world-seeking/ambitious”), followed by apposition/qualification مرد دلیر (“a brave man”). Adjectival elements follow the nouns in this classical style.
segments.s369-g68: چو پیل دژآگاه و چون نره شیر uses similes introduced by چو (“like/as”). پیل (elephant) is followed by the compound adjective دژآگاه (literally ‘fortress-aware’ / ‘fortress-warden’), then و (and), then another simile چو + نره شیر (“like a male lion”). This is a series of comparisons; each چو governs the following noun phrase.
segments.s369-g69: همی گشت بر گرد مردان چین: همی + گشت gives a habitual/continuous past sense (“kept turning/was turning”). بر گرد is a prepositional expression meaning “around,” governing مردان چین (“the Chinese men”). Word order: adverbial particle — verb — prepositional phrase — object.
segments.s369-g70: تو گفتی همی بر نوردد زمین is reported speech introduced by تو گفتی (“you said”). In classical Persian the clause reported after گفتی often uses a subjunctive/present form to express future-in-the-past; همی before نوردد gives iterative or emphatic aspect. بر نوردد زمین = “would traverse/overrun the earth” (نوردد here = traverse/roll over).
segments.s369-g71: بکشت از گوان جهان شست مرد contains archaic verbal forms. بکشت is a prefixed past-form verb used in classical prose/poetry (here functioning as a past action). از گوان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase (گوان = ‘such/like’/classical particle), جهان شست is a compound meaning literally “washed the world” (شست = past of ‘wash’, used figuratively to mean ‘cleansed/wiped the world of …’), and مرد is the direct object “men.” The sequence follows older Persian syntax where verbs and their objects can be arranged for emphasis.
segments.s369-g72: دران تاختنها به گرز نبرد: دران (“in that/therein”) introduces the locative setting. تاختنها is a plural verbal noun (gerund) from تاختن, here “the charges / the mounted attacks.” به گرز uses به as an instrumental/locative preposition meaning “with/by (a) mace,” and نبرد is the noun “battle/struggle.” The phrase means that in those charges he fought in battle using a mace.
segments.s369-g9: به پیش صف چینیان ایستاد is a prepositional phrase + verb: به پیش + noun phrase (صفِ چینیان) marks position 'in front of / before the Chinese line', with the verb ایستاد (past, 3rd sg.) coming after the phrase as is normal in Persian narrative word order.
segments.s369-g10: خداوند بهزاد را کرد یاد is a main clause with explicit object-marking را: خداوند (subject, 'the lord/sovereign') + بهزاد را (definite object) + کرد یاد (literal 'made remembrance' = 'remembered'); کرد is the light verb and یاد is the nominal 'remembrance'.
segments.s369-g11: کدامست گفت از شما شیردل combines a question word کدامست ('which is it?') with گفت (past 'said') introducing reported speech; از شما here functions partitive/possessionally ('of you' = 'which of you'), and شیردل is an epithet ('lion‑hearted', vocative/identifying the addressee).
segments.s369-g12: که آید سوی نیزه جان گسل is a relative/nominal clause introduced by که ('who/that'); آید is a present/simple-future sense 'comes/will come', سوی is the preposition 'toward', and نیزه جان‌گسل is a poetic compound where جان‌گسل ('life‑splitting' / 'soul‑rending') modifies نیزه 'spear'.
segments.s369-g13: کجا باشد آن جادوی خویش کام is a rhetorical question: کجا ('where') + باشد (subjunctive/optative/third‑person copula used in rhetorical phrasing) + آن جادوی خویش ('that magic of his' — جادوی is the noun 'magic/sorcery', خوی + ش = 'his nature / his own') + کام ('desire').
segments.s369-g14: کجا خواست نام و هزارانش نام repeats the question adverb کجا and uses خواست (past of خواستن, 'sought/wanted') with نام ('name' here meaning 'fame/reputation') as its object; هزارانش is 'his thousands' (هزاران + possessive -ش) modifying the second نام, forming a parallel question about desire for fame and countless renown.
segments.s369-g15: برفت آن زمان پیش او نامخواست places the past action first: برفت (past 'he departed/withdrew' — narrative past) + آن زمان ('at that time') as a temporal adverbial + پیش او ('before him/in his presence') + نامخواست, here used as a past lexical compound meaning 'did not (or did not merely) seek a name/fame' or 'refused to seek fame' (نام + خواست), so the clause says he left then without seeking renown.
segments.s369-g16: تو گفتی که همچو ستونست راست is reported speech: تو گفتی ('you said') + که introducing the content clause, and همچو ستونست راست is the predication 'is like a pillar, upright' — ستونست is a fused form ('is a pillar' = ستون + است) and راست here strengthens the sense 'straight/upright' or 'truly'.
segments.s369-g33: گرامی is a substantive/adjective meaning “honoured” or “noble” used as the subject. بدید is the simple past (they/he saw) in epic register. آن درفش is the definite object phrase “that banner”; چو نیل is a simile appended to describe the banner (چو = “like/as”, نیل here = the indigo/dark-blue colour). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object with the simile following the object.
segments.s369-g34: که introduces a relative clause modifying "that banner" ("which ..."). افگنده is a past participle meaning “thrown/let down” and بود(ند) here functions as the auxiliary that forms the past perfect-like construction (افگنده بودند = “they had let down”). از پشت = “from the back (of)”; پشت پیل = “the elephant’s back” (پیل = elephant).
segments.s369-g35: فرود آمد is a compound past verb meaning “he descended / alighted” (فرود = descent, آمد = came). و links the actions. بر گرفت is a past action “seized / took up” with بر marking direction/on (i.e. took it up onto his hand). آن is the object pronoun “it/that.” ز (poetic variant of از) + خاک = “from the ground/dust.”
segments.s369-g36: بیفشاند is an epic/past-form verb meaning here “he shook off / scattered (the dust).” The phrase از خاک specifies what was shaken off (“from the dust/earth”); خاک in this clause has the narrower sense “dust.” و links the next verb. بسترد is an older/formal past verb (from سترد) meaning here “he wiped/covered/cleared” and پاک is an adverb/adjective “clean/pure” describing the result (i.e. ‘wiped it clean’).
segments.s369-g37: چو = “when.” او را contains the pronoun او with the object marker را, so بدیدند is plural past “they saw him.” گردان چین is a noun phrase describing the observing force: گردان “troop/company” and چین here means “fold/array” (i.e. rank/array of troops). Word order places the temporal clause first and the subject phrase after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s369-g38: که introduces a descriptive phrase—"that" (آن) specifying the item: نیزه is “spear.” نامدار is an adjective “renowned, famous.” گزین here functions as an adjective/participle meaning “chosen / select” (literally ‘select/chosen’), qualifying the spear.
segments.s369-g39: ازان (poetic contraction of از + آن) = “from that / from it.” خاک = “the earth/ground.” برداشت is a past verbal noun/past action here meaning “they lifted / removed” (برداشت = took up). بسترد again is a past verb from سترد meaning here “covered/wrapped/cleared” and و برد continues the sequence “and carried off / bore away” — برد is used in the sense of having taken/gained possession (epic register). The three verbs form a tight sequence of actions on the object.
segments.s369-g40: به گردش گرفتند is a compound meaning “took it into a circuit / set it to turning” (به = to/toward, گردش = circuit/rotation, گرفتند = they took/seized). مردان گرد = “the men around/about” (مردان = men/warriors, گرد = around/about or ‘the surrounding ones’); together the phrase describes the surrounding men seizing it and wheeling about. Word order is verb before the agent phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s369-g17: بگشتند is a past plural verb 'they turned' (verb before noun here because of poetic inversion). هر دو modifies سوار to mean 'both riders'; هژیر is a proper name in apposition to or specifying one of those riders. The clause therefore says that the two horsemen (including Hojir) turned.
segments.s369-g18: The repeated preposition به introduces instruments/means: 'with/by (the) mace and by the spear, by the sword and (by) the arrow.' و coordinates the items; the phrase lists the weapons they take up.
segments.s369-g19: گرامی is an epithet/title 'the noble/honored one'; گوی here functions as part of the descriptive phrase (an older/poetic element); بود is the past copula 'was'. با زور شیر is an instrumental/comparative phrase 'with the strength of a lion,' giving the manner of his might.
segments.s369-g20: نتابید is past negative of تابیدن here meaning 'did not endure/withstand.' با او marks the adversary 'against him,' and سوار دلیر ('the brave rider') is the opposing figure; the clause says the brave rider could not be withstood (i.e., the other did not hold against him).
segments.s369-g21: گرفت is a simple past 'he seized/took'; از + noun marks the source 'from the noble.' نبرده is a negative past participle 'not taken/undiminished' modifying گریغ ('pride, haughtiness'), so the sequence says he seized away the noble's unbending pride (poetic/elliptical phrasing).
segments.s369-g22: This is a nominal clause with بود as the copula 'was': گرامی (the noble) + کفش (boot/shoe) — i.e. the noble's boot — بود (was) برنده تیغ, where برنده is an adjective 'cutting/sharp' and تیغ 'blade/sword.' Together the clause compares the boot to a cutting blade.
segments.s369-g23: خرامید is a past-tense verb 'he strode/pranced' (a proud, measured step). گرامی is the subject 'the noble'; با خشم تیز is an adverbial phrase 'with keen/sharp anger,' where تیز modifies خشم.
segments.s369-g24: دل is 'heart' as subject; از introduces the source 'from/ because of' (از کینه کشتگان = 'from the hatred of the slain'). پر is the predicate 'full (of)' and ستیز is a noun 'strife/fighting' — 'his heart was full of strife because of the hatred for the slain.'
segments.s369-g41: ز هر سو (a prepositional phrase) = 'from every side'; به گردش is an adverbial phrase 'in a circuit/around' placed before the verb; همی is the archaic/poetic imperfective marker that gives a continuous or habitual past sense; تاختند is past‑plural 'they rushed/charged' and is the main verb. The word order (adverbials before verb) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s369-g42: به شمشیر marks the instrument/means ('with/by swords') and precedes the object; دستش is the noun + possessive suffix 'his hand' (دست + ش). بینداختند is past‑plural 'they threw/hurled' (bi‑ + انداختند) and takes the object دستش — so the clause means they flung swords toward/at his hand. Note the preposition به used here to indicate instrument or direction.
segments.s369-g43: درفش فریدون is a possessive noun phrase 'Fereydun's banner' where the ezafe is omitted for poetic brevity; به دندان گرفت literally 'took in the teeth' (به introduces the instrument/location), with گرفت (past singular) 'he seized/took' as the verb. The subject is implied from context (singular).
segments.s369-g44: همی زد is the continuous/habitual past 'he kept striking' (همی + زد). به یک دست 'with one hand' uses به + noun to indicate instrument/way. گرز‌ای شگفت is a poetic form: گرز (mace) + ای where ای functions like an older/metrical form of the indefinite/genitive marker (instead of گرزی); شگفت (wonderful) modifies گرز. The phrase as a whole: 'he struck with a wondrous mace held in one hand.'
segments.s369-g45: سرانجام is the sentence‑initial adverb 'finally/at last'. کارش uses the noun کار with the possessive suffix ش ('his case/affair' — here referring to his fate or person) and functions as the logical object; بکشتند is past‑plural 'they killed'. زار follows as a predicative/adverbial adjective meaning 'wretched/miserable' or denoting a pitiable state that accompanies the action (i.e. he was killed in a pitiable way).
segments.s369-g46: بران ('upon that' / 'thereon') begins the clause; گرم modifies خاکش — 'his warm dust' (خاک + ش = 'his dust', i.e. his still‑warm remains). فگندند is past‑plural 'they cast/threw', and خوار is an adjectival/resultative adverb meaning 'despised/treated with contempt'. The clause describes the humiliating treatment of the corpse: 'they threw his warm dust upon it, treating him contemptuously.' Note the possessive suffix on خاکش and the inverted, compact word order typical of epic diction.
segments.s369-g47: دریغ is an exclamatory interjection 'alas/pity'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. نبرده here functions adjectivally/participially to mean 'not taken/captured' (archaic form: negated perfect/participle) modifying سوار هژبر 'the rider Hezbar'; the sequence expresses lament that this rider was not seized. The construction is compact and somewhat archaic/poetic.
segments.s369-g48: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that'. بازش is باز 'again' with the object suffix ش 'him' (i.e. 'him again'); ندید is past 'did not see' (negative with ن‑). آن خردمند پیر is a noun phrase 'that wise old man' (آن demonstrative + two adjectives). Together: 'for the wise old man did not see him again.'
segments.s369-g57: به + زیر اندرون is a directional phrase: به (towards/into) + زیر اندرون (the inner quarters). تیزرو (an adjectival epithet 'swift') and شولکی (a small cloak) form a condensed nominal phrase describing the rider — Persian often strings such modifiers together without a copula, so this whole phrase functions as the moving subject/figure that 'darted in'. Word order is poetic (modifier cluster after the directional phrase).
segments.s369-g58: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. نبود is the past negative of بودن ('was not'), asserting nonexistence/uniqueness. چنان 'such' qualifies the following verbless comparison, and the poetic order از هزاران یکی ('of thousands one') means 'one of thousands' — Persian allows inversion for rhythm. Together: 'such that there was not one of thousands (like him)'.
segments.s369-g59: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'to/at that' (thereon), and تیره modifies آوردگاه (literally 'dark/bleak battlefield'); the noun آوردگاه 'place of bringing' here means 'battlefield'. The phrase is a compact past-tense clause 'he came to that dark field'.
segments.s369-g60: به (here functioning adverbially, 'with/by') + آواز ('voice') forms the adverbial phrase 'in a loud voice / aloud'. گفت would normally be the past verb 'he said', but the provided noun senses do not include the verb sense, so it is used here as the speech verb. ای is the vocative 'O', and گزیده سپاه is the addressee 'chosen/elite host' (گزیده = elite, سپاه = host/army).
segments.s369-g61: کدامست is an interrogative fusion (کدام است) 'which is it?' functioning as the question word. مرد از شما 'man of you / among you' places the group-marker از شما after 'mard' (poetic placement). نامدار is a predicate adjective 'renowned/famed'. The whole clause asks 'Which man among you is renowned?'
segments.s369-g62: This is a string of epithets describing that man: جهاندیده 'world-wise/experienced' + و (and) + گرد + و (and) + نیزه‌گزار 'spear-bearer'. The adjectives are piled in series (typical in epic diction). One listed lemma (گرد) does not match the senses provided in the data, so it is left unspecified below.
segments.s369-g63: که begins a relative/interrogative clause 'who (is he) that...'. پیش من 'in my presence / before me' uses پیش as a locative/prepositional adverb. آیند is a present/subjunctive form 'come' (used here as 'will come' / 'come forward'), and نیزه به دست is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with spear in hand' (به + noun 'دست' with the instrument 'نیزه').
segments.s369-g64: که again introduces an explanatory statement. امروز 'today' and در پیش (literally 'in front/presence') set the time and place; مرد 'a man' is the subject, and آمدست is an archaic/perfect-form compound (آمد + است) meaning 'has come' — the enclitic '-ست' is the copula 'is/has'. The clause states that 'today a man has come before (me)'.
segments.s369-g1: بیامد is the simple past (3rd singular) 'came' placed at the start by poetic inversion. سر سروران سپاه is a nominal cluster where سر = 'head/chief' governs سرورانِ سپاه ('the commanders of the army'); the usual ezafe linking is omitted as typical in classical verse.
segments.s369-g2: This phrase identifies the person named: پسر = 'son' takes the following names as its apposition (here 'تهم جاماسپ' functions as a proper name), and دستور شاه = 'the king's order' (دستور modified by شاه in genitive). Classical Persian often drops the overt ezafe; relation is understood from context.
segments.s369-g3: نبرده is the negative past participial form meaning 'had not borne/was not possessed of' (verb appears before its objects in the poetic order). سواری = 'rider/horseman' and گرامیش نام = 'so honoured a name' (گرامیش = 'honoured' modifying نام = 'name/reputation'); the adjective and noun are compacted without an ezafe in verse.
segments.s369-g4: به ماننده = 'in the manner of / like' (به + ماننده functioning as a prepositional simile). پور = 'son (of)' attaches to the following name دستان (here the father's name) and then سام is the personal name; classical compression again omits explicit genitive markers.
segments.s369-g5: یکی introduces an indefinite 'one / a certain one.' چرمه‌ای = 'a leather (garment)' (the -ای ending makes it an adjectival/indefinite form) and برنشسته is a past participle 'seated on / mounted upon' that follows its noun in this poetic order; سمند = 'steed/warhorse' is the object of برنشسته.
segments.s369-g6: Again یکی = 'another one.' گام زن is a compound meaning 'one who strides / a stepper' (گام = 'step', زن here functions to form an agentive compound), and باره بی‌گزند = 'bearing a load unharmed' where باره = 'load/burden' and بی‌گزند = 'without harm / unharmed.'
segments.s369-g7: چماننده = 'like / resembling' introduces a simile. چرمه modifies the following noun (a leather garment), نونده = 'composer/creator' (here 'singer/poet/composer') and جوان = 'young (man)'; the whole phrase means 'like a young composer clad in leather.'
segments.s369-g8: یکی = 'one' (introducing the first of two comparisons). کوه پاره = literally 'a piece torn from a mountain' (کوه = 'mountain', پاره = 'piece/torn fragment'), ست = the copula 'is', and گوی روان = 'a moving/rolling sphere' (گوی = 'sphere/ball' used metaphorically, روان = 'moving/flowing'), producing a vivid metaphorical description.
segments.s369-g25: میان صف دشمن اندر فتاد is arranged as a locative phrase followed by a past-tense verb: میان صف دشمن (in the midst/among the enemy ranks) + اندر (archaic preposition reinforcing 'in/among') + فتاد (past of افتادن, 'fell'). فتاد is used intransitively here — 'fell/ plunged into' — and the whole phrase means 'fell into the enemy line.'
segments.s369-g26: پس is an adverb 'then'; از دامن کوه is an ablative phrase 'from the dāman (skirt/foothills) of the mountain' (دامن کوه = mountain's skirt/foothills). برخاست is the past 'rose/arose' and باد is the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion): 'a wind arose.'
segments.s369-g27: سپاه (the host/army) is the subject; از دو رو ('from two faces/sides') is an ablative phrase indicating direction; بر هم is a compound meaning 'against one another / upon each other'; آویختند is past plural 'they clung/locked/engaged' used figuratively for 'they clashed/engaged one another.' Word order: subject + directional phrase + verb at end.
segments.s369-g28: و is the coordinating 'and'; گرد here means 'dust' (a cloud of dust kicked up); از دو لشکر 'from the two hosts/armies' specifies the source; برانگیختند is past plural 'they raised/caused to rise' (i.e., they raised dust). The object (گرد) is placed before the source phrase for poetic ordering.
segments.s369-g29: بدان is the archaic/poetic compound (= به + آن) meaning 'in/at that'; here it introduces 'that turmoil.' اندر میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the host/army.' The whole chunk means 'in that turmoil among the army.' Note: دان here is part of the fossilized demonstrative 'بدان' (poetic 'به آن').
segments.s369-g30: ازان = 'from that' (referring back to the turmoil). زخم گردان is best read as 'the wounds of the troops' (زخم modified by گردان 'troop, company' as a genitive relation), and گرد سیاه is 'black dust' (گرد = dust/cloud, سیاه = black). و links the two items produced by the turmoil.
segments.s369-g31: بیفتاد (past of افتادن) 'fell' is the main verb; از دست ایرانیان is an ablative phrase 'from the hands/of the possession of the Iranians' — together meaning 'fell out of Iranian hands' (i.e., was lost by them). The subject (what fell) is supplied by the following segment.
segments.s369-g32: درفش فروزنده کاویان is a three-part noun phrase: درفش 'banner/standard' modified by the adjective فروزنده 'radiant/illuminating', with کاویان (Kāvyān) as the genitive 'of the Kayanids' (i.e., 'the radiant banner of the Kayanids'). Persian ezāfe links are omitted in poetry but understood.

sentences.s369-s45: The meadows and plains all turned tulip‑coloured; blood kept flowing over the plains and open country.
sentences.s369-s46: That battlefield was so (full) from the many slain that one could hardly travel the road.
sentences.s369-s37: At last an arrow of the heavens came to him; it had thus come forth from the sky.
sentences.s369-s38: That fine-colored little cloak fell away; he died and did not rise — such was the end of the battle.
sentences.s369-s39: Alas for that noble rider too, who was cast down for nothing.
sentences.s369-s40: He was like his father and his equal — alas for that fair-faced, high-ranking man.
sentences.s369-s25: Then Bostūr the lion also came — the untaken/undefeated, of the Kian line, son of Zarīr.
sentences.s369-s26: He slew him — one of those countless enemies who were cast in ill fortune.
sentences.s369-s27: At last he returned victorious and joyful; he came before his father and stood.
sentences.s369-s28: Then that chosen horseman came; afterwards the world's king, Newzār.
sentences.s369-s41: When that famed horseman was slain, thousands upon thousands from the ranks set themselves in motion.
sentences.s369-s42: They fell upon one another in every corner; from the surface of the ground they raised dust.
sentences.s369-s43: This fighting arose and lasted two sevens (two weeks); of them not a single horseman slept for a while.
sentences.s369-s44: The lands became full of the slain and the weary; the inner tents, too, were shut.
sentences.s369-s33: The Chinese horsemen charged at him, and they set about throwing him down.
sentences.s369-s34: The world-seeking rider, a brave man, like an elephant—alert as a fortress-warden—and like a male lion.
sentences.s369-s35: He kept circling around the Chinese men; you said he would traverse the earth.
sentences.s369-s36: He slew men and washed the world (with them); in those charges he fought in battle with a mace.
sentences.s369-s5: He stood before the Chinese ranks; the lord remembered Behzad.
sentences.s369-s6: He asked, 'Which one of you, lion‑hearted, will come toward the life‑splitting spear?'
sentences.s369-s7: Where, then, is that magic of his desire? Where did he seek a name — and his thousands of names?
sentences.s369-s8: At that time he withdrew before him and did not seek a name; you said that he is, like a pillar, upright.
sentences.s369-s17: The honoured one saw that banner, dark as indigo, which they had let down from the elephant's back.
sentences.s369-s18: He descended and took it up from the ground; he shook off the dust and wiped it clean.
sentences.s369-s19: When the arrayed host saw him — that famed, chosen spear —
sentences.s369-s20: They lifted it from that ground, wrapped/covered it and carried it off; the men about took it and went around in a circuit.
sentences.s369-s9: Both horsemen — Hojir among them — turned, taking up mace and spear, sword and arrow.
sentences.s369-s10: The noble was strong as a lion; the brave horseman could not withstand him.
sentences.s369-s11: He seized from the noble the stubborn pride; the noble's boot was sharp as a blade.
sentences.s369-s12: The noble strode proudly with keen anger; his heart, from hatred for the slain, was full of strife.
sentences.s369-s21: They rushed in a circle from every side and hurled their swords at his hand.
sentences.s369-s22: He took Fereydun's standard between his teeth and kept striking with a wondrous mace in one hand.
sentences.s369-s23: At last they killed him; they hurled his still‑warm dust upon it and treated him with contempt.
sentences.s369-s24: Alas that the rider Hezbar was not captured — for the wise old man did not see him again.
sentences.s369-s29: A swift rider in a short cloak darted down into the inner quarters — there was not one like him among thousands.
sentences.s369-s30: He came to that dark battlefield and, raising his voice, cried, 'O chosen host!'
sentences.s369-s31: Which among you is the famed man — world‑wise, gallant, and spear‑bearer?
sentences.s369-s32: Who will come before me with spear in hand? For today a man has come into my presence.
sentences.s369-s1: The chief of the army's commanders, the son of Tahm‑Jamasp, came at the king's command.
sentences.s369-s2: No rider had borne so honourable a name, like Sam, son of Dastan.
sentences.s369-s3: One, clad in leather, was mounted on a steed; another strode along, bearing his burden unharmed.
sentences.s369-s4: Like a leather-clad young composer, — one is a fragment torn from a mountain, a rolling sphere in motion.
sentences.s369-s13: He fell into the ranks of the enemy; then a wind rose from the skirt (foothills) of the mountain.
sentences.s369-s14: The host from two sides rushed upon one another, and dust rose from the two armies.
sentences.s369-s15: In that tumult amid the army came the troops' wounds and black dust.
sentences.s369-s16: The radiant banner of the Kayanids fell from the hands of the Iranians.
